coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-embedded ZIF-8 nanocomposite for fluorescence sensing of Cr(VI) anion and picric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qinglan
Chen, Siping
Ding, Caijie
Zhao, Suqing
Pang, Jiahong
Yan, Wenfu
coredata.dc:description: An ingenious fluorescence sensor was constructed by in-situ encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to resist aggregation-caused quenching of CDs. The obtained CDs@ZIF nanocomposite showed excellent sensing abilities towards Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid with quenching constant values of 7.08 × 104 M−1 and 1.50 × 105 M−1, respectively. The fluorescent responsiveness of CDs@ZIF nanocomposite for Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid can be ascribed to the inner filter effect.

ZIF-8
Nanocomposites
Sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthetic route of CDs@ZIF, (b) TEM image of CDs, (c) TEM image, (d) SEM image, (e) size distribution, (f) XRD pattern, (g) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm of CDs@ZIF.

(a) Fluorescence titration of CDs@ZIF for K2Cr2O7, (b) correlation between the I0/I-1 and concentration of K2Cr2O7, (c) fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF in solutions of different acid anion, (d) fluorescence titration of CDs@ZIF for picric acid, (e) correlation between the I0/I-1 and concentration of picric acid, (f) UV–vis spectra of K2Cr2O7 and picric acid, emission and excitation spectra of CDs@ZIF.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-embedded ZIF-8 nanocomposite for fluorescence sensing of Cr(VI) anion and picric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biomedical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ingenious fluorescence sensor was constructed by in-situ encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to resist aggregation-caused quenching of CDs. The obtained CDs@ZIF nanocomposite showed excellent sensing abilities towards Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid with quenching constant values of 7.08 × 104 M−1 and 1.50 × 105 M−1, respectively. The fluorescent responsiveness of CDs@ZIF nanocomposite for Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid can be ascribed to the inner filter effect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With long-term development of industry including electroplating, leather manufacturing and mining industry, metal ions and organic pollutants have been undesirably discharged, thereby seriously threatening environment and human health [1,2]. Cr2O7 2–anion as a typical toxic heavy-metal pollutant, could induce respiratory inflammation, liver injury, acute renal failure, and cancer due to strong oxidation of functional biomolecules. The maximum allowable concentration of 0.05 mg/L for Cr(VI) is stipulated in the sanitary standards for drinking water (GB5749-2022). In addition, picric acid has been widely used as raw material of explosives, fireworks, pesticides, and acid dye. Even at a low concentration, it could result in dermatitis, conjunctivitis, bronchitis, diarrhea, fever, liver and kidney injury. According to the surface water environmental quality standard (GB3838-2002), the maximum acceptable concentration of picric acid is 0.5 mg/L. Therefore, development of efficient, sensitive and rapid detection methods is very critical to strictly control the content of these pollutants in water.

Nowadays, various methods have been used to detect metal and organic pollutants, such as surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [3], fluorescence spectroscopy [4], and colorimetry [5]. Among them, fluorescence sensing with advantages of high sensitivity, low cost and convenience, has drawn more and more interests in the field of trace detection of pollutants. Diversified fluorescence sensors have been fabricated based on small molecular fluorophores [6], metal nanoclusters [7], quantum dots [8], and metal–organic frameworks [9]. On account of easy synthesis, good photostability and biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) are widely used to construct fluorescence sensors. While most CDs demonstrate aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) property, showing weak fluorescence in concentrated and solid state. Embedding CDs in the solid-state matrix materials (e.g. transparent polymer [10], silica [11], montmorillonite [12]) is an effective strategy to resist the ACQ effect. In this work, we utilized the restriction effect of zeolitic imidazolate framework towards CDs to hinder its fluorescence quenching. The fabricated CDs@ZIF nanocomposite was further developed as a fluorescence sensor for efficient detection of Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid.

CDs was firstly prepared according to the synthetic procedure as described in the Supplementary Information. Then the mixture of poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (0.15 mL), CDs solution (5.00 mL), and 2-methylimidazole (0.82 g) was stirred for 10 min. 3.00 mL of zinc acetate solution (1 mmol/L) was added into the mixture, and milky white suspension immediately appeared. The mixture was further stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Finally, CDs@ZIF was obtained after being centrifuged and washed by deionized water and ethanol.

After adequately ultrasonic treatment, a dispersed solution of CDs@ZIF (2 mL, 1 mg/mL) was poured into a quartz cuvette. Fluorescence spectrum of the dispersion was firstly measured under excitation of 366 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm was marked as I0. The fluorescence titration detection was then performed by continuously adding 2 μL of K2Cr2O7 solution (10 mmol/L) into CDs@ZIF suspension, and the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm were recorded and marked as I. The correlation between the I0/I-1 and concentration of K2Cr2O7 followed the Stern-Volmer equation: I0/I − 1 = KSV × [K2Cr2O7]. KSV represented the quenching constant.

CDs prepared from citric acid and polyethyleneimine exhibited good water dispersibility, ranging in size from 1.9 to 3.5 nm (Fig. 1 a, 1b and S1). CDs were then embedded into ZIF-8 through in-situ encapsulation method by employing poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA) as structure-directing agent. The obtained CDs@ZIF nanocomposite demonstrated a sphere shape and rough surface with average size of 807 nm (Fig. 1c, d and e). Obvious defects on the surface were attributed to the instable interaction of 2-methylimidazole with PDDA during the rapid growth of crystals. Fig. 1f showed characteristic diffraction peaks at 7.31, 10.40, 12.70, 14.72, 16.44, 18.04, which were assigned to (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), and (222) diffractions of ZIF-8, suggesting high crystallinity of CDs@ZIF. Meanwhile, N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm analysis indicated its large surface area of 1,446 m2/g (Fig. 1g). The pore-size distribution of CDs@ZIF was concentrated at 1.0 and 1.5 nm calculated by using N2 - DFT model. Good water-dispersibility of CDs@ZIF was demonstrated by the concentrated hydrated particle size distribution (Fig. S2).

Excitation spectra of CDs and CDs@ZIF were presented in Fig. S3. CDs solution emitted blue fluorescence at 453 nm under the excitation of 319 nm (Fig. S4). Fluorescence emission intensity of CDs solution became stronger after the addition of deionized water, indicating its ACQ property. After being encapsulated, blue fluorescent CDs@ZIF with optimal excitation wavelength at 366 nm was demonstrated by its excitation spectrum and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S5). CDs@ZIF exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of 7 %. Besides of the redshift for the optimal excitation wavelength, zeolitic imidazolate framework reversed the fluorescence property of CDs from ACQ to aggregation-induced emission, resulting in stronger fluorescence with increasing concentrations of CDs@ZIF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence titration of the CDs@ZIF suspension was carried out by adding different amounts of K2Cr2O7. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF at 451 nm gradually declined with increasing concentration of Cr2O7 2- from 0 to 100 uM. The relationship between the quenching efficiency of CDs@ZIF nanocomposite sensor and the analyte concentration was shown in the Stern-Volmer curve (Fig. 2b). There was a good linearity of I/I0-1 versus the Cr2O7 2− concentration with the KSV value of 7.08 × 104 M−1. Furthermore, the fluorescence responses of CDs@ZIF towards Cr2O7 2−, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, Cl-, Br-, I- and their mixture were also investigated. The effective and sensitive detection for Cr2O7 2− without any interference from other anions was demonstrated in Fig. 2c.

In order to explore the sensing ability of CDs@ZIF towards picric acid, similar fluorescence titration of CDs@ZIF suspension was further performed. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF decreased gradually with the addition of picric acid (Fig. 2d). A good linear relationship between I/I0-1 and picric acid concentration was shown in the Stern-Volmer curve, and the quenching constant KSV was 1.50 × 105 M−1 (Fig. 2e). There are certain decreases in fluorescence intensity for CDs@ZIF after addition of 2,4-dinitrophenol, 4-nitrophenol, 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitroaniline. While the fluorescence intensity weakened the most after adding picric acid (Fig. S6). Compared with the reported fluorescent materials in Table S1, CDs@ZIF exhibited comparable limit of detection and quenching constant for Cr2O7 2- and picric acid.

The detection mechanism of CDs@ZIF nanocomposite sensor for Cr2O7 2− and picric acid was further clarified through fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. As depicted in Fig. 2f, Cr2O7 2− and picric acid exhibited absorption peaks at 355 nm, and CDs@ZIF demonstrated an optimal excitation wavelength of 366 nm. There are partial overlaps between the absorption spectra of analytes and the excitation spectrum of CDs@ZIF, as well as its emission spectrum. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching responses of CDs@ZIF towards Cr2O7 2− and picric acid were resulted from the inner filter effect [13].

A novel fluorescent CDs@ZIF sensor was successfully fabricated by in-situ encapsulation method for the detection of Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid. CDs@ZIF showed rapid and sensitive fluorescence quenching properties toward Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid with the KSV values of 7.08 × 104 M−1 and 1.50 × 105 M−1, respectively. Furthermore, CDs@ZIF sensor exhibited good anti-interference against other anions. CDs@ZIF can serve as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid attributed to the inner filter effect. This work provides an efficient and facile method for the construction of fluorescent sensor using CDs with defective aggregation-caused quenching property, and highlights its multiple and sensitive detection for Cr2O7 2- anion and picric acid.

Qinglan Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Siping Chen: Investigation, Methodology. Caijie Ding: Investigation. Suqing Zhao: Supervision. Jiahong Pang: Investigation. Wenfu Yan: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021A1515110331) and Guangzhou Science and Technology Foundation (202201010308) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.135464.

ce:section.ce:para:
